questions.q: A 5 kg object experiences a net force of 20 N. Calculate its acceleration, and identify how it changes if the mass is doubled with the same force.
options: 4 m s⁻², unchanged when mass doubles
4 m s⁻², halves to 2 m s⁻² when mass doubles
100 m s⁻², doubles when mass doubles
0.25 m s⁻², doubles when mass doubles
questions.explanation: By Newton's second law a = F_net/m = 20/5 = 4 m s⁻². For a fixed force, acceleration is inversely proportional to mass, so doubling the mass to 10 kg halves the acceleration to 2 m s⁻². The second law contains both proportionalities — acceleration up with force, down with mass.

questions.q: Distinguish between mass and weight, and calculate the weight of a 12 kg object on Earth (g = 9.8).
options: Mass and weight are both 12 kg
Mass is 12 kg (scalar, constant); weight is 117.6 N (vector, = mg)
Mass is 117.6 N; weight is 12 kg
Weight is 12 kg; mass is 117.6 N
questions.explanation: Mass is the scalar amount of matter (kg), constant everywhere; weight is the gravitational force on it (N), a vector equal to mg that varies with location. Weight = 12 × 9.8 = 117.6 N downward. The trap is giving weight in kilograms — weight is a force, in newtons.

questions.q: A box is pushed at constant velocity across a rough floor. Identify the net force on it and how the applied force compares with friction.
options: Net force is forward; applied force exceeds friction
Net force is zero; applied force exactly equals friction
Net force is backward; friction exceeds applied force
Net force equals the weight; friction is zero
questions.explanation: Constant velocity means zero acceleration, so by Newton's first and second laws the net force is zero — the applied force exactly balances friction (equal magnitude, opposite direction). The common error is thinking you need a bigger force than friction to keep something moving; a bigger force would cause acceleration, not constant velocity.

questions.q: Explain why an action-reaction pair of forces never cancels.
options: Because the reaction force is smaller
Because the two forces act on different objects, not the same one
Because they act at different times
Because one is a contact force and one is a field force
questions.explanation: By Newton's third law, if A pushes on B then B pushes equally and oppositely on A — but the action acts on B and the reaction on A. Since only forces on the same object determine its motion, an action-reaction pair (on two different objects) cannot cancel. This is why walking works: you push back on the ground, and the ground pushes you forward.

questions.q: A 3 kg block slides from rest down a frictionless 25° incline. Calculate its speed after sliding 4 m (g = 9.8, sin 25° ≈ 0.423).
options: 8.1 m s⁻¹
5.7 m s⁻¹
4.1 m s⁻¹
2.9 m s⁻¹
questions.explanation: Frictionless incline: a = g sin θ = 9.8 × 0.423 ≈ 4.1 m s⁻² (mass cancels). Then suvat (time missing): v² = u² + 2as = 0 + 2 × 4.1 × 4 ≈ 33, so v ≈ 5.7 m s⁻¹. The 3 kg mass is a distractor — it cancels on a frictionless incline. (4.1 is the acceleration, not the speed.)

questions.q: A 2 kg block slides from rest down a rough 30° incline (slope length 5 m, friction 4 N). Using energy, calculate its kinetic energy at the bottom (g = 9.8).
questions.explanation: Vertical height = 5 × sin 30° = 2.5 m, so PE lost = mgΔh = 2 × 9.8 × 2.5 = 49 J. Work done against friction = 4 × 5 = 20 J (transformed to heat). KE at bottom = 49 − 20 = 29 J. This matches the forces method (net force 5.8 N → a = 2.9 m s⁻² → v ≈ 5.4 m s⁻¹), confirming energy and forces agree.

questions.q: A 1000 kg car at 20 m s⁻¹ collides with a stationary 1500 kg car and they lock together. Calculate the kinetic energy transformed in the collision.
options: 0 J
questions.explanation: Momentum: 1000 × 20 = 2500v → v = 8 m s⁻¹. KE before = ½ × 1000 × 20² = 200 000 J; KE after = ½ × 2500 × 8² = 80 000 J. Transformed = 200 000 − 80 000 = 120 000 J (to heat, sound, crumpling). Momentum gives the velocity; an energy comparison gives the energy lost — the signature collision method.

questions.q: A 1000 kg car at 30 m s⁻¹ brakes to a stop with a braking force of 6000 N. Calculate the stopping distance using the work-energy theorem.
questions.explanation: KE to remove = ½ × 1000 × 30² = 450 000 J. The work-energy theorem gives braking force × distance = KE: 6000 × d = 450 000 → d = 75 m. No acceleration or time is needed. Note KE ∝ v², so doubling the speed quadruples the stopping distance — why speed limits matter.

questions.q: A 0.2 kg ball is dropped from 3 m. Using energy then impulse, calculate its impact speed and the impulse to stop it (g = 9.8).
options: Speed 7.7 m s⁻¹; impulse 1.5 kg m s⁻¹
Speed 5.9 m s⁻¹; impulse 1.2 kg m s⁻¹
Speed 7.7 m s⁻¹; impulse 0.4 kg m s⁻¹
Speed 3.8 m s⁻¹; impulse 1.5 kg m s⁻¹
questions.explanation: Energy: v = √(2gΔh) = √(2 × 9.8 × 3) = √58.8 ≈ 7.7 m s⁻¹ (mass cancels). Impulse to stop = change in momentum = mΔv = 0.2 × 7.7 ≈ 1.5 kg m s⁻¹, directed opposite to the motion. This braids conservation of energy (episode 3) with impulse (episode 4) in one question.

questions.q: A 2 kg trolley at 5 m s⁻¹ hits a stationary 3 kg trolley; afterward the 2 kg trolley moves forward at 1 m s⁻¹. Calculate the 3 kg trolley's velocity and classify the collision.
options: 2.7 m s⁻¹; elastic (KE conserved)
2.7 m s⁻¹; inelastic (KE not conserved)
4.0 m s⁻¹; inelastic
1.3 m s⁻¹; elastic
questions.explanation: Momentum: 2 × 5 = 2 × 1 + 3v → 10 = 2 + 3v → v ≈ 2.7 m s⁻¹. KE before = ½ × 2 × 5² = 25 J; KE after = ½ × 2 × 1² + ½ × 3 × 2.7² ≈ 1 + 11 = 12 J. Since 12 J < 25 J, KE is not conserved — the collision is inelastic, with ~13 J transformed. Momentum finds the velocity; comparing KE classifies the collision.
